target.title: fix(doctor): suppress --fix trailer when no pending config changes remain
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw doctor` (without `--fix`) prints `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` on every run, even when nothing in the run actually had a pending config change. After a successful `--fix` settles the workspace, a follow-up plain `doctor` invocation still emits the trailer, training operators to ignore a non-actionable cue.
- Why it matters: the trailer is the "you have something to apply" signal. When it fires on a clean state, it makes `Doctor complete.` ambiguous — users either re-run `--fix` for nothing or learn to ignore the line everywhere, including the cases where it does mean something.
- What changed: thread the already-tracked `pendingChanges` flag from `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` through the doctor config result, and gate the trailer in `runWriteConfigHealth` on it. The trailer now only emits when the config flow actually flagged at least one pending repair the user could apply.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no behavior change for `--fix` runs, no behavior change when `pendingChanges` is truly set, no edits to other doctor panels or contributions, no rename/move.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX (CLI diagnostics output)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80435
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: the unconditional `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` trailer that `runWriteConfigHealth` emits whenever `--fix` is not passed, regardless of whether anything was actually pending.
- Real environment tested: local macOS (Darwin 24.6.0, Node v24.10.0) checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` at `upstream/main`, run with `pnpm dev doctor`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
  - `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:changed` (lint core + tsgo core + tsgo core tests + import-cycles + boundary guards)
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-trailer-test pnpm dev doctor --fix --non-interactive` (× 2 to settle)
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-trailer-test pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` (the previously failing invocation)
- Evidence after fix:
  - Unit lane: `Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 11 passed (11)` — includes the two new regression cases for `doctor:write-config`.
  - `pnpm check:changed`: exits 0 with `Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.` on lint, clean typecheck on `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:core:test`, `0 runtime value cycle(s)` from `check:import-cycles`.
  - Settled clean re-run ends with `└  Doctor complete.` and no trailing `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` line.
- Observed result after fix: on the clean re-run, the trailer is gone; runs that genuinely have pending repairs queued continue to print it (covered by the second new unit test).
- What was not tested: I did not reproduce the exact source-checkout + entrypoint-mismatch host layout from the bug report on this machine (no spare gateway-install/migration setup), so the live trailer-on-clean-state observation comes from a fresh `OPENCLAW_HOME` rather than the reporter's npm-over-pnpm upgrade scenario. The function-level regression test mirrors the same code path either way.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `runWriteConfigHealth` reached its `else` branch whenever `shouldWriteConfig` resolved to `false`, and then emitted the trailer based only on whether `--fix` was missing. There was no signal of whether any earlier health contribution would have *had* something to apply, so the message printed even on a fully settled run.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no unit test asserting that the trailer is suppressed on a clean run. The new `doctor:write-config — skips the doctor --fix trailer when no pending config changes remain` test closes that gap.
- Contributing context (if known): `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` already tracks `pendingChanges` locally for `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow`, but it wasn't surfaced on the returned `DoctorConfigResult`, so downstream contributions had no way to consult it.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - `doctor:write-config` skips the `--fix` trailer when `configResult.pendingChanges === false` and `prompter.shouldRepair === false`.
  - `doctor:write-config` still prints the `--fix` trailer when `configResult.pendingChanges === true` and `prompter.shouldRepair === false`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely in the single branch in `runWriteConfigHealth`; exercising both `pendingChanges` values against the live `doctor:write-config` contribution is enough to lock in the gate without standing up the whole doctor flow.
- Existing test that already covers this: none — the existing tests in this file assert ordering and the legacy update-skip helper, not trailer emission.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor` (no `--fix`) no longer prints `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` on runs where no health contribution flagged a pending config change. Runs that do have pending changes are unchanged.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
doctor --non-interactive (clean state) -> Doctor complete. + "Run openclaw doctor --fix to apply changes."

After:
doctor --non-interactive (clean state) -> Doctor complete.
doctor --non-interactive (pending change) -> Doctor complete. + "Run openclaw doctor --fix to apply changes."
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0), reporter saw it on Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.10.0 (reporter: Node v22.22.2)
- Model/provider: N/A — pure doctor CLI diagnostics
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): a settled `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` where the previous `--fix` exited without `Updated ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`

### Steps

1. `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive` until it exits cleanly with no `Updated ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` line.
2. `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` immediately afterward.
3. Observe the trailer.

### Expected

The settled re-run ends with `└  Doctor complete.` and no trailing `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` line.

### Actual (pre-fix)

The settled re-run ends with `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` followed by `└  Doctor complete.`, even though no panel reported a pending change.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing-then-passing unit assertions covering both `pendingChanges` branches (`src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`).
- [x] Trace/log snippet from `pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` on settled `OPENCLAW_HOME` showing `Doctor complete.` with no trailer.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - Settled clean re-run of `pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` against a freshly populated `OPENCLAW_HOME` ends with `Doctor complete.` and no trailer.
  - `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` — 11 passed, including the two new cases.
  - `pnpm check:changed` — green on lint core, runtime sidecar loader guard, import-cycles, webhook body guard, pairing guards, changelog attributions, dup coverage, `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:core:test`, oxlint core.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `pendingChanges === undefined` (older test mocks / future callers) — falsy, trailer suppressed; backwards compatible with existing inline-context tests in this file.
  - `pendingChanges === true && shouldRepair === false` — trailer still prints (covered by the second new unit test).
  - `pendingChanges === true && shouldRepair === true` — function takes the write branch, never reaches the trailer.
- What I did **not** verify: a live reproduction of the original Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64 layout with `auditGatewayServiceConfig` returning a non-empty issue list, because that requires the reporter's npm-over-pnpm upgrade setup.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — `pendingChanges` is added as an optional field on `DoctorConfigResult`; existing mocks/test fixtures that omit it observe the previous "no trailer" outcome they already had when nothing was pending.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a future contribution might want the trailer to fire on a state that isn't surfaced via `configResult.pendingChanges`.
  - Mitigation: any new pending state should be threaded through `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` (now trivial — just OR it into the existing local `pendingChanges`). The regression test locks the contract for `runWriteConfigHealth`.

AI-assisted: drafted with Claude Code; human-run real behavior proof and unit test verification on local checkout.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

PR surface: Source +66, Tests +502, Docs +12, Other 0. Total +580 across 15 files.

target.title: fix(env): propagate service-managed GH_TOKEN to trusted exec
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `~/.openclaw/.env` could not be used as a durable source for operator-curated tool secrets like `GH_TOKEN` because override-only-dangerous keys were stripped before the managed gateway service could expose them to trusted exec children. - Why it matters: operators can configure `gh` and similar CLI tools for OpenClaw, but after gateway install/restart those tools could not see `GH_TOKEN` through the managed service path, so authenticated CLI workflows failed unless the token was injected some other way. - What changed: allow `~/.openclaw/.env` to retain override-only-blocked secrets as managed service keys, add a trusted exec inherited-env allowlist sourced from `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`, and document the durable service env behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): everywhere-dangerous env vars such as `LD_PRELOAD`, `NODE_OPTIONS`, `BASH_ENV`, `DYLD_*`, and `GIT_DIR` stay blocked. `OPENCLAW_*` keys still do not gain trusted exec inheritance. Untrusted exec postures do not receive these secrets. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `GH_TOKEN` placed in `~/.openclaw/.env` should survive managed gateway install/restart and be available to trusted gateway exec children, so `gh` can authenticate without manually injecting the token into each command. - Real environment tested: macOS OpenClaw gateway installed as a managed service, local OpenClaw checkout from v2026.5.2 with this patch applied, `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`, Telegram/agent exec path using `host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```sh cp ~/.env ~/.openclaw/.env chmod 600 ~/.openclaw/.env openclaw gateway stop openclaw gateway install --force openclaw gateway start openclaw gateway status ``` then ask the agent to run `gh auth status` and report back - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Telegram screenshot showing the live exec environment reporting `GH_TOKEN_present: true`, `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`, `gh auth status` logged in via `GH_TOKEN`, and `gh api user` returning the authenticated account JSON. Account details and token values are redacted. - Observed result after fix: trusted gateway exec can use `GH_TOKEN` inherited from the managed service env, and `gh` authenticates successfully from the OpenClaw exec environment. - What was not tested: Linux/systemd real-service behavior was not manually tested; the Linux path is covered by the shared service-env/unit tests. Non-trusted exec postures were verified by targeted unit tests, not by a second live service screenshot. The exec integration test (`bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts`) is skipped on Windows (shell-dependent); unit-level coverage runs on all platforms. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - `before-1.jpg` and `before-2.jpg` — terminal screenshots showing the pre-fix managed-service behavior where the gateway env path stripped `GH_TOKEN` before it could reach the exec child, so `gh` could not authenticate after service install/restart. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `~/.openclaw/.env` parsing treated keys that are dangerous as agent request overrides the same as keys that are dangerous everywhere. This stripped operator-curated tool tokens like `GH_TOKEN` before the managed service could record them as durable env. Separately, exec env sanitization had no trusted-mode inherited-key allowlist, so there was no narrow path for managed service keys to reach trusted tool children. - Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered dangerous env stripping and service env formatting, but did not cover the desired operator-curated secret path from `~/.openclaw/.env` through managed service metadata into trusted gateway exec. - Contributing context (if known): the security model already distinguished inherited/env override risk, but the durable service env path did not preserve that distinction. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts` - `src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts` - `src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts` - `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `GH_TOKEN` and similar override-only-blocked keys can be retained from owner-controlled durable service env, become managed service keys, and reach exec children only in the trusted gateway posture (`host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`). Unsafe keys and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover each policy seam, and the exec integration test proves the env-composition wiring reaches the child process under the relevant exec postures without requiring live GitHub credentials in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A, new tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can place `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env`, reinstall/start the managed gateway, and use `gh` from trusted gateway exec. This does not grant those tokens to untrusted exec postures. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser strips override-only-dangerous key -> managed gateway service has no GH_TOKEN-managed allowlist entry -> trusted exec child cannot authenticate gh After: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser keeps operator-curated override-only key -> install records GH_TOKEN in OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS -> trusted gateway exec allowlist permits inherited GH_TOKEN -> gh authenticates from the exec child ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this intentionally allows operator-curated service env secrets like `GH_TOKEN` to reach trusted gateway exec children. The path is constrained to keys recorded by the managed service env metadata and to the trusted exec posture. Everywhere-dangerous keys are still stripped, `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded, request overrides remain sanitized, and non-trusted postures do not inherit the allowlisted secret. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local managed OpenClaw gateway service - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw agent exec path, GitHub CLI (`gh`) as the real CLI consumer - Relevant config (redacted): `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`; `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` included `GH_TOKEN`; token value redacted in all evidence. ### Steps 1. Put `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env` and set owner-only permissions. 2. Reinstall/restart the managed gateway service with this patch applied. 3. Run a trusted gateway exec env probe and `gh auth status` / `gh api user`. 4. Run the targeted regression tests: ```sh pnpm test src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts ``` ### Expected - `process.env.GH_TOKEN` is present inside trusted gateway exec. - `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` contains `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` reports authentication via `GH_TOKEN`. - Untrusted exec postures do not receive the fixture token in tests. - Everywhere-dangerous and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. ### Actual - Live evidence showed `GH_TOKEN_present: true` and `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`. - `gh auth status` authenticated via `GH_TOKEN` and `gh api user` succeeded with redacted account output. - Targeted tests covered the parser, managed service allowlist, host env sanitizer, daemon install plan, and exec wiring. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Evidence files prepared for upload: - `before-1.jpg` - `before-2.jpg` - `after-1.jpg` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Managed gateway install/restart picks up `GH_TOKEN` from `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Trusted gateway exec sees `GH_TOKEN` and `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` includes `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` and `gh api user` work from the OpenClaw exec environment using `GH_TOKEN`. - Targeted tests exercise trusted, allowlist, and prompted exec postures plus dangerous-key exclusions. - Edge cases checked: - `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded from trusted inherited allowlist. - Everywhere-dangerous keys remain blocked even if listed. - PATH override smuggling remains blocked. - Default sanitizer behavior remains strict without `allowInheritedKeys`. - What you did **not** verify: - Manual Linux/systemd live service verification. - Every provider token named in the override-only dangerous list; `GH_TOKEN` is the live proof case and tests use representative fixture keys. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for operators who want this behavior. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: place `GH_TOKEN` or similar operator-curated tool secret in `~/.openclaw/.env`, ensure the file is owner-only, then reinstall/restart the managed gateway service so `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` is regenerated. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: secrets that were previously stripped from managed service env can now reach trusted exec children. - Mitigation: only operator-curated durable service env keys are considered, only trusted gateway exec posture receives the inherited allowlist, and dangerous-everywhere plus `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Risk: operators may assume all exec postures receive `GH_TOKEN` after placing it in `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Mitigation: docs state that the exec layer decides inheritance by posture and that these keys are not auto-propagated to untrusted commands.

PR surface: Source +257, Tests +279, Docs +40, Generated 0. Total +576 across 14 files.

target.title: feat(mattermost): add automatic ack reactions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Mattermost supported typing, preview streaming, and manual `message action=react`, but it did not send automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- Why it matters: Mattermost was the main chat-channel outlier versus Discord, Slack, Telegram, Matrix, and WhatsApp for the baseline "seen / working on it" feedback path.
- What changed: added Mattermost automatic ack reactions, Mattermost emoji-name normalization for common unicode defaults, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` / `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides, and the matching generated config-surface artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no Mattermost lifecycle status reactions, no changes to typing behavior, and no changes to draft preview streaming.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: automatic Mattermost ack reactions for accepted inbound messages, including config override resolution and optional ack cleanup after reply.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw maintainer worktree on Linux with Mattermost-focused tests, build, generated-config checks, the full changed-scope gate, and a live Mattermost workspace smoke on this exact branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reactions.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/ack-reactions.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
  - `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
  - `pnpm config:docs:check`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  20 passed (20)

pnpm build -> passed
pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata -> passed
pnpm config:docs:check -> OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256
pnpm check:changed -> passed

Live Mattermost smoke on this branch -> passed
- started the branch with `pnpm gateway:watch`
- configured `messages.ackReactionScope=all` and `channels.mattermost.ackReaction=eyes`
- sent real Mattermost messages in the connected workspace
- observed visible ack reactions in Mattermost
- no ack/reaction failure strings appeared in the runtime logs
```
- Observed result after fix: Mattermost now has an automatic ack reaction path wired into inbound monitoring, common unicode ack emoji are normalized to Mattermost emoji names, config/docs/generated surfaces are aligned, and visible ack reactions were confirmed in a live Mattermost workspace on this exact branch.
- What was not tested: exhaustive live coverage for uncommon/custom unicode emoji names.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Mattermost only supported manual `message action=react`; there was no automatic inbound ack reaction path in the monitor.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A. This is a scoped feature/parity improvement, not a regression fix.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the first implementation pass missed the generated bundled channel metadata and config baseline hash that need to move with a public config-surface change.
- Contributing context (if known): Mattermost has a preview-streaming-first design, so this change intentionally stops at the ack layer instead of adding full status-reaction lifecycle UI.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/ack-reactions.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: account/channel/global ack resolution, mention-scope gating, emoji normalization, and `removeAckAfterReply` cleanup behavior.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the existing Mattermost websocket monitor harness hangs in this worktree, so the smallest stable coverage is the new ack helper unit coverage plus generated-surface/build/changed-gate proof.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reactions.test.ts` now covers emoji normalization through the reaction transport.
- If no new test is added, why not: a monitor-level websocket assertion was attempted and dropped because the existing harness hangs; I kept stable unit coverage rather than land a flaky test.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Mattermost can now add automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- `messages.removeAckAfterReply` now applies to Mattermost ack reactions too.
- Mattermost now supports optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` and `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides.
- Common unicode ack emoji like `👀` are normalized to Mattermost emoji names automatically.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[inbound Mattermost message] -> [mention/access gating] -> [typing + preview streaming] -> [reply]

After:
[inbound Mattermost message] -> [mention/access gating] -> [auto ack reaction] -> [typing + preview streaming] -> [reply] -> [optional ack removal]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - The new network calls are the existing Mattermost reaction add/remove API calls, sent only for accepted inbound messages when ack reactions are enabled. They are gated by normal Mattermost access checks, `messages.ackReaction*`, and `channels.mattermost.actions.reactions`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node 24 worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted): `messages.ackReaction`, `messages.ackReactionScope`, `messages.removeAckAfterReply`, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction`

### Steps

1. Configure `messages.ackReaction` and `messages.ackReactionScope` for Mattermost.
2. Send an accepted inbound Mattermost message that passes normal access/mention gating.
3. Observe ack reaction creation, then send a reply path that optionally removes the ack when `removeAckAfterReply` is enabled.

### Expected

- Mattermost adds the configured ack reaction when the inbound message qualifies.
- Common unicode defaults normalize to Mattermost emoji names.
- Ack cleanup only happens after a visible reply is delivered.

### Actual

- Verified via new unit coverage, generated-surface checks, build, `pnpm check:changed`, and a live Mattermost smoke on this exact branch that showed visible ack reactions plus live ack removal after visible replies.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

target.title: feat(android): add chat agent selector
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Android chat surface could show multiple agents indirectly, but users had no explicit way to choose which configured gateway agent should receive new chat messages.
- Why it matters: multi-agent OpenClaw setups need an obvious mobile path for switching between agents such as `main` and local agents without relying on existing session history or hidden session-key behavior.
- What changed: the Android runtime now exposes gateway agent summaries to chat UI state, and the chat sheet renders an agent selector chip row above the session/thread selector.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway API contract changes, no new Android permissions, no authentication changes, and no changes to session/thread listing semantics beyond switching the active phone chat session key when an agent is selected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Android Chat now displays selectable agent chips when the connected gateway reports more than one agent, allowing mobile users to switch between agents before sending messages.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw gateway with multiple configured agents (`main` plus a local Ollama-backed agent), Android debug build generated from this branch and sideloaded/tested on a physical Android phone.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Build Android debug APK from this branch.
  2. Install/sideload the debug APK on the Android phone.
  3. Connect the app to the local OpenClaw gateway.
  4. Open Chat and verify the agent selector row appears above the session chips.
  5. Select the alternate agent and send a chat message.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - Screenshot from the physical Android phone after this patch: ![Screenshot_20260510-154513.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0637284d-e61c-41b8-a0bb-edf635a94893)
  - Manual runtime verification on a real Android phone confirmed the selector appeared and worked.
  - Copied live build/lint output from the patched branch is included below.
- Observed result after fix: Chat showed agent selector chips and selecting another agent switched the chat target for subsequent messages.
- What was not tested: automated UI screenshot tests and full end-to-end CI device tests were not added.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Android Chat exposed session chips only; there was no first-class agent selector in the Chat sheet.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): multi-agent gateway data was already fetched for the home canvas, but it was not exposed to the Chat UI.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
 - [ ] Unit test
 - [ ] Seam / integration test
 - [ ] End-to-end test
 - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: N/A
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: this is a small UI wiring change using existing gateway `agents.list` data already fetched by `NodeRuntime`; it was manually verified on a real device and covered by Android lint/build gates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Android Chat now shows an agent selector row when the connected gateway reports multiple agents. Tapping an agent chip switches the active phone chat target to that agent’s node session before sending messages.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[open Android Chat] -> [session/thread chips only] -> [no direct agent choice]

After:
[open Android Chat] -> [agent chips + session/thread chips]
                    -> [tap agent chip]
                    -> [chat target switches to selected agent]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux gateway host; Android physical device
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw gateway, Android debug APK built from this branch
- Model/provider: multi-agent setup with `main` and local Ollama-backed agent
- Integration/channel (if any): Android node app over gateway connection
- Relevant config (redacted): gateway configured with multiple agents; sensitive tokens/IPs omitted

### Steps

1. Build the Android debug APK from this branch.
2. Install/sideload the APK on an Android phone connected to an OpenClaw gateway with multiple agents configured.
3. Open the Android app Chat surface.
4. Confirm agent chips appear above session/thread chips.
5. Tap a non-default agent chip and send a message.

### Expected

- Android Chat provides a visible way to select the desired gateway agent.
- Selecting an agent switches the active phone chat target to that agent.
- Existing session/thread selection still works below the agent selector.

### Actual

- Agent chips render above session/thread chips when multiple agents are available.
- Selecting another agent switches the chat target and messages route to that agent.
- Existing chat session controls remain available.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)
![Screenshot_20260510-154513.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0637284d-e61c-41b8-a0bb-edf635a94893)


Copied live output from the patched branch:

```text
> Task :app:ktlintCheck

BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 8s
13 actionable tasks: 2 executed, 11 up-to-date
```

```text
> Task :app:assemblePlayDebug

BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 1s
38 actionable tasks: 4 executed, 34 up-to-date
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Android debug APK built successfully from this branch.
  - Android Chat agent selector appeared in a real multi-agent setup.
  - Selecting the alternate agent switched the active chat target before sending.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Selector is hidden when there is only one or zero reported agents.
  - Existing session/thread selector remains available below the new agent selector.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full Android instrumentation/E2E test suite.
  - Every possible agent naming/emoji combination.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the extra chip row could add vertical density to the Chat sheet on small screens.
 - Mitigation: the row uses the same compact horizontal-scroll chip pattern as the existing session selector and is hidden unless multiple agents are available.
- Risk: selecting an agent could be confused with selecting a session/thread.
 - Mitigation: the agent selector is rendered as a separate row above the existing session chips and uses the gateway agent name/emoji where available.

target.title: fix(/v1/responses): accept text field on requests for OpenAI SDK 6.x parity
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `/v1/responses` rejects every request from clients using the current OpenAI Node SDK (`openai` >= 6.x) or `@langchain/openai` >= 1.x with `Bad request - please check your parameters: Unrecognized key: "text"`. The OpenAI SDK auto-populates a top-level `text: { format: { type: "text" } }` field on every Responses-API request, but `CreateResponseBodySchema` is `.strict()` and does not include `text`.
- Why it matters: this breaks n8n's AI Agent (Tools Agent V3) and any other downstream client using a current OpenAI SDK against OpenClaw's "OpenAI-compatible" Responses endpoint. The endpoint is effectively unusable from those clients today.
- What changed: added `text` to `CreateResponseBodySchema` with an OpenAI-spec-shaped object — `format` is a discriminated union over `text` / `json_schema` / `json_object`, plus optional `verbosity`. Schema acceptance only; no runtime behavior change.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): `openresponses-http.ts` agent dispatch, prompt build, or output handling. Only the request schema. `format.type === "json_schema"` and `"json_object"` parse successfully but are not yet enforced at runtime; that belongs in a follow-up PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `/v1/responses` returns HTTP 400 `Unrecognized key: "text"` when called by any current OpenAI Node SDK (`openai` 6.37.0 in this case, via `@langchain/openai` 1.1.3 inside n8n 2.19.5).
- Real environment tested: macOS 15 (Darwin 25.4.0), OpenClaw `2026.5.4` running as the dedicated `openclaw` LaunchDaemon, gateway on `127.0.0.1:18789` with `gateway.auth.mode: token` and `gateway.http.endpoints.responses.enabled: true`. n8n 2.19.5 self-hosted via `npx n8n`, AI Agent node `@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain.agent` v3.1.
- Exact steps or command run: enabled the `responses` endpoint, configured an n8n OpenAI credential against `http://localhost:18789/v1` with the gateway bearer token, wired an AI Agent (Tools Agent V3) with model `openclaw`, executed the workflow.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture from this Mac):

Before (live, against the production-installed OpenClaw on this machine):

```
$ curl -s -X POST http://localhost:18789/v1/responses \
    -H \"authorization: Bearer \$TOKEN\" -H \"content-type: application/json\" \
    -d '{\"model\":\"openclaw\",\"input\":\"hi\",\"text\":{\"format\":{\"type\":\"text\"}}}' \
    -w \"\\nHTTP %{http_code}\\n\"
{\"error\":{\"message\":\": Unrecognized key: \\\"text\\\"\",\"type\":\"invalid_request_error\"}}
HTTP 400
```

n8n surfaces the same as a `LangChain MODEL_NOT_FOUND` (404 wrapper) on the AI Agent node:

```
NodeOperationError: The resource you are requesting could not be found
  at executeBatch (.../@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain/.../ToolsAgent/V3/helpers/executeBatch.ts:113:11)
  at ExecuteContext.toolsAgentExecute (.../ToolsAgent/V3/execute.ts:46:66)
```

After (patched schema parsed in isolation against the same request bodies a current OpenAI SDK / n8n sends):

```
$ node --experimental-strip-types -e \"...import patched schema...\"
ACCEPT   auto-populated default (n8n / openai SDK 6.x)
ACCEPT   json_schema structured output
ACCEPT   verbosity only
REJECT   invalid format type
           Invalid discriminator value. Expected 'text' | 'json_schema' | 'json_object'
REJECT   unknown top-level field (regression check)
           Unrecognized key: \"notARealField\"
```

- Observed result after fix: requests that previously 400'd at the schema layer now parse successfully; the strict-validation contract on the rest of the body is preserved (still rejects unknown top-level keys and invalid format types).
- What was not tested: full n8n round-trip against the patched gateway. The patched OpenClaw build was validated via the schema in isolation; swapping the deployed npm-bundled `openresponses-http-*.js` on the live install and re-running the n8n workflow is the next verification step but requires replacing a globally-installed bundled file (out of scope for this PR's evidence). Unit + parity tests cover the schema contract.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `CreateResponseBodySchema` declared `.strict()` and the `text` field was not in its allowed-keys list. OpenAI's Responses API spec defines `text.format` (and a newer `text.verbosity`), and the `openai` Node SDK >= 6.x auto-populates `text: { format: { type: \"text\" } }` on every request even when no structured output is requested. The strict schema rejects that auto-populated field, returning HTTP 400 before the request reaches the agent runtime.
- Missing detection / guardrail: parity tests only covered fields previously sent by Codex / direct curl probes; no test exercised the request shape produced by an unmodified `openai` SDK or `@langchain/openai` instance at the versions n8n ships with.
- Contributing context: the existing schema already has a documented \"Phase 1: ignore but accept these fields\" group (`temperature`, `top_p`, `metadata`, `store`, `previous_response_id`, `reasoning`, `truncation`). `text` belongs in the same accept-but-don't-yet-honor bucket for OpenAI-SDK parity.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/open-responses.schema.test.ts` (new, colocated). 10 cases pinning the `text` contract: default `text` accepted, `json_schema` accepted, `json_object` accepted, `verbosity` only, empty `text`, unknown `format.type` rejected, `json_schema` missing required fields rejected, unknown top-level keys on `text` rejected, plus a regression case asserting the rest of `CreateResponseBodySchema` stays strict, and a full-shape happy path matching what an OpenAI SDK 6.x client sends today.
- Scenario the test should lock in: the gateway must accept the request body shape any current OpenAI SDK / LangChain ChatOpenAI 1.x emits, while still rejecting unknown top-level fields and malformed `text.format` payloads.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the regression is a schema-level contract violation; a colocated zod-schema unit test is the cheapest place to catch it. A live HTTP test would also catch it but adds gateway boot, auth, and agent setup cost for no extra signal.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none. `openresponses-parity.test.ts` covers `input_image`, `input_file`, and tool-definition shape but not the OpenAI-SDK-emitted `text` field.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Requests carrying `text` (any OpenAI-SDK >= 6.x or `@langchain/openai` >= 1.x client) no longer fail with HTTP 400 at the schema layer.
- Default `text.format.type === \"text\"` is the only format honored at runtime; no change to current default-text output behavior.
- `text.format.type === \"json_schema\"` and `\"json_object\"` are accepted by the schema but silently produce regular text output; runtime enforcement is intentionally left for a follow-up since it requires changes to the agent dispatch path.
- No new defaults, no config keys.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A — schema-only change.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

The change is purely a request-validation widening on an already-authenticated, already-feature-flagged endpoint (`gateway.http.endpoints.responses.enabled`). No new fields are persisted, logged, or forwarded; non-text formats are silently treated as text.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 (Darwin 25.4.0)
- Runtime/container: native macOS install via `npm i -g openclaw` (LaunchDaemon as user `openclaw`)
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` (default agent), accessed via the `openclaw` model id over the `/v1/responses` endpoint
- Integration/channel: HTTP via `n8n` 2.19.5 AI Agent (Tools Agent V3) using `@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain` 3.1, `@langchain/openai` 1.1.3, `openai` 6.37.0
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  \"gateway\": {
    \"auth\": { \"mode\": \"token\", \"token\": \"<48-char redacted>\" },
    \"http\": {
      \"endpoints\": {
        \"chatCompletions\": { \"enabled\": true },
        \"responses\":       { \"enabled\": true }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Enable `gateway.http.endpoints.responses.enabled: true` and restart the gateway.
2. `curl` the endpoint with the SDK-default body shape:
   `curl -X POST http://localhost:18789/v1/responses -H 'authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -H 'content-type: application/json' -d '{\"model\":\"openclaw\",\"input\":\"hi\",\"text\":{\"format\":{\"type\":\"text\"}}}'`

### Expected

- HTTP 200 with a Responses-shaped JSON body (`object: \"response\"`, `output[].content[].text`).

### Actual (before this PR)

- HTTP 400 with `{\"error\":{\"message\":\": Unrecognized key: \\\"text\\\"\",\"type\":\"invalid_request_error\"}}`. n8n surfaces this as a LangChain `MODEL_NOT_FOUND` 404 on the AI Agent node and the workflow halts.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (terminal capture above)
- [x] Trace/log snippets (n8n stack trace above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm test src/gateway/open-responses.schema.test.ts` — 10/10 pass.
  - `pnpm test src/gateway/openresponses-parity.test.ts src/gateway/openresponses-phase.test.ts src/gateway/open-responses.schema.test.ts` — 34/34 pass.
  - `pnpm check:changed` — green (lint, runtime sidecar guard, import cycles, webhook/auth lints).
  - `pnpm check:test-types` — green (`tsgo:core:test` and `tsgo:extensions:test`).
  - `pnpm format:check` on every changed file — green (auto-formatted with `oxfmt`).
  - `pnpm build` — clean.
  - `pnpm check:changelog-attributions` — green.
  - Schema parity proof: parsed the SDK-default request body, a `json_schema` request, a `verbosity`-only request, an invalid `format.type`, and an unknown top-level key against the patched schema; first three accepted, last two rejected with clear messages.
- Edge cases checked: empty `text` object, `verbosity` without `format`, malformed `json_schema` (missing `name`/`schema`), unknown top-level key on `text`, unknown top-level key on the request (regression check that the rest of the schema stays `.strict()`).
- What I did **not** verify:
  - End-to-end n8n round-trip against a swapped-in patched gateway build. Doing that requires replacing the bundled `openresponses-http-*.js` in a global npm install; I left it for a follow-up rather than tampering with the user's running OpenClaw install.
  - Runtime enforcement of `text.format.type === \"json_schema\" | \"json_object\"` (intentionally out of scope; requires agent-dispatch changes and likely a separate design discussion).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — strictly widens what the schema accepts; nothing previously accepted is now rejected.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: clients that send `text.format.type === \"json_schema\"` may now expect structured-output enforcement that the runtime does not yet provide, and silently receive plain text.
  - Mitigation: documented this in the schema comment and in CHANGELOG so the limitation is discoverable; runtime enforcement can be added in a follow-up PR without further schema changes. The default OpenAI SDK auto-populated case (`text.format.type === \"text\"`) is the dominant real-world request shape today and is fully honored.

target.title: refactor(auto-reply): drop dead /reset branches in fast-path session init
target.body:
## Summary

This PR was originally proposed as a fix for #80377, claiming `/reset` was preserving session-level overrides incorrectly. After review by `clawsweeper` and inspection of `initSessionState` (the actual production reset path), it turns out the runtime behavior is **intentional**:

- behavior overrides (thinking, verbose, reasoning, ttsAuto) are deliberately preserved across `/reset` so users don't re-enable them every time (see `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts:510-518`)
- model/provider/auth overrides go through `resolveResetPreservedSelection`, which preserves user-driven selections and clears only auto-created ones (per #69301)

So this PR has been **pivoted from a behavior fix to a cleanup-only refactor**. The original commit (`1a1685a`) and the regression tests it added have been reverted in `1d70235`. The only remaining net change versus `origin/main` is replacing nine trivial `resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.X : existingEntry?.X` ternaries in `initFastReplySessionState` with the simple `existingEntry?.X` expression.

**Net effect: zero semantic change vs `origin/main`.** The dead `resetTriggered` branches no longer mislead readers into thinking the fast-path bootstrap has reset-specific logic that the runtime doesn't actually have.

Issue #80377 should be closed as wontfix / by-design (the bundle pattern that was reported is a copy-paste artifact in this fast-path init, not a production runtime bug). This PR does **not** fix #80377; it only references it.

## Files touched

- `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-fast-path.ts` — simplify 9 no-op ternaries (no semantic change)

## Behavior proof (no-op refactor)

This is a semantic no-op cleanup; the proof is the diff itself. Before/after, every assignment evaluates to the same value:

```ts
// Before (one of nine identical patterns)
thinkingLevel: resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.thinkingLevel : existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
// After
thinkingLevel: existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
```

For both `resetTriggered === true` and `resetTriggered === false`, both expressions yield `existingEntry?.thinkingLevel`. The `resetTriggered` semantics still flow through the `...(!resetTriggered ? existingEntry : undefined)` spread on line 245 (unchanged), so reset still drops `responseUsage` and `sessionStartedAt` correctly. The full pre-existing test suite for `get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` (12 tests) continues to pass:

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  12 passed (12)
```

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` → 12/12 (pre-existing tests, unchanged)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core` → green
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core:test` → green (with `GOMEMLIMIT=2GiB` on a memory-constrained dev box)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: improve: simplify PR context and evidence
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The PR template and external-contributor body gate were still tied to a rigid real-behavior-proof form. That made readable PRs harder to write and conflated two separate concerns: author-provided PR context versus ClawSweeper's stronger proof-sufficiency review.

## Why This Change Was Made

Replace the field-level proof form with authored `What Problem This Solves` and `Evidence` sections, and make Barnacle manage only the missing-context label `triage: needs-pr-context`. OpenClaw no longer emits or clears ClawSweeper-owned proof labels such as `proof: supplied`, `proof: sufficient`, or `proof: override`.

Companion ClawSweeper draft: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/pull/319

## User Impact

Authors can write concise PRs that explain intent, design, impact, and the most useful validation evidence. Reviewers and AI can use that context while relying on the diff, tests, CI, and ClawSweeper review as the source of truth for correctness and proof sufficiency.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/real-behavior-proof-policy.test.ts test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts`
  - 245 tests passed.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check` across all changed OpenClaw files.
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --parallel-tests "<focused tooling command>"`
  - Clean: no accepted or actionable findings.
- ClawSweeper companion validation: `pnpm run check` passed.

target.title: fix(media): warn when MEDIA: token is skipped inside fenced code block (#41966)
target.body:
## Problem

When an LLM wraps a `MEDIA:` token inside a triple-backtick code fence, `splitMediaFromOutput()` correctly skips extraction (by design — code examples shouldn't trigger delivery). However, this creates a **silent failure**: the user sees the raw `MEDIA:` path as plain text and receives no media, with no indication of what went wrong.

As reported in #41966, LLMs like GLM-4.7-Flash, Claude, and GPT-4 regularly wrap file paths in code fences, especially when the default `TOOLS.md` template shows `MEDIA:` inside a code block as an example.

## Fix

Emit a `logWarn()` when a `MEDIA:` line is found **inside** a fenced code block. This is a **warn-only** change:

- ✅ No behavior change to extraction logic
- ✅ No new config flags  
- ✅ No changes to `mediaUrls` output
- ✅ Visible text output unchanged (fenced `MEDIA:` lines still render as text)

Warning message shown in logs:
```
media: MEDIA: token skipped — it is inside a fenced code block and will not be delivered. Remove the surrounding backticks/fence to send this as media.
```

## Tests

Three new test cases under `"MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966)"`:

1. **Warns** on a triple-backtick fenced `MEDIA:` line
2. **Does NOT warn** for `MEDIA:` tokens outside fences (no regression)  
3. **Warns once per fenced MEDIA line**; both lines still appear as visible text (contract unchanged)

TypeScript: clean (`tsc --noEmit` passes).

## Changelog

```
[fix] Emit logWarn when MEDIA: token is silently skipped inside a fenced code block (#41966)
```

## Related

Follows up on my [analysis comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/41966#issuecomment-4416096864) proposing Option B (warn-only) as the safe minimal fix before any potential Option A (extract-from-fences) config flag.

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** When an LLM wraps a `MEDIA:` token inside a triple-backtick fenced code block, `splitMediaFromOutput()` silently skips the token (by design — code examples must not trigger media delivery) but emits no log signal, so the user sees a raw `MEDIA:` path in chat and has no clue why nothing was delivered. This PR (closes #41966) adds a `logWarn()` so the silent skip becomes diagnosable, with no behavior change to extraction or visible output.

**Real environment tested:**
- Date: 2026-05-27T21:13:03Z
- Host: Darwin 25.4.0 arm64 (macOS, Apple Silicon)
- Node: v25.5.0
- Repo: `fix/41966-media-token-warn-in-fence` @ `5102c3ca43` (Bartok9/openclaw fork)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
git checkout fix/41966-media-token-warn-in-fence
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --prefer-offline
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.media.config.ts \
  src/media/parse.test.ts \
  -t "MEDIA token inside fenced code block" \
  --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/bartokmoltbot/clawd/openclaw

 ✓  media  src/media/parse.test.ts > splitMediaFromOutput > MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966) > warns when a MEDIA: token appears inside a triple-backtick fence 23ms
 ✓  media  src/media/parse.test.ts > splitMediaFromOutput > MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966) > does not warn when a MEDIA: token appears outside a fence 1ms
 ✓  media  src/media/parse.test.ts > splitMediaFromOutput > MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966) > warns once per fenced MEDIA line and still keeps the line as visible text 0ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed | 57 skipped (60)
   Start at  17:12:52
   Duration  200ms (transform 80ms, setup 80ms, import 18ms, tests 26ms, environment 0ms)
```

Full file run (all 60 cases including the 3 new ones) also passes — 1 file passed, 60/60 tests green at 17:12:43 in 451ms (transform 364ms, setup 233ms, import 50ms, tests 73ms). Confirms no regression in the other `splitMediaFromOutput` behaviors that share `parse.ts`.

**Observed result after fix:**

The three new test cases under `"MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966)"` exercise the actual `splitMediaFromOutput()` function (no mocks of the unit under test; only `logWarn` is spied on). Case 1 asserts `logWarn` is called with the `"media: MEDIA: token skipped — it is inside a fenced code block…"` message when `MEDIA:` appears between triple backticks. Case 2 asserts `logWarn` is **not** called when `MEDIA:` appears outside any fence (no regression in the happy path). Case 3 asserts each fenced `MEDIA:` line emits exactly one warn, and that the `mediaUrls`/visible-text contract is unchanged (the fenced line still renders as text, no media is lifted). Before this patch the function would skip silently with no log signal; after the patch the warn is observable.

**What was not tested:**

- End-to-end Telegram/Slack rendering of the warn (this is a `logWarn()` change; the test is at the function boundary)
- Localization of the warn string (the string is in English; matches existing `logWarn` conventions in `parse.ts`)
- Behavior with non-triple-backtick fences (4-backtick, tilde, indented code) — out of scope per the issue (`#41966` is specifically about triple-backtick wrapping by LLMs)


---

## Update 2026-06-13 — rebased onto current main

Rebased onto `upstream/main` @ `340c2456bb` to clear the conflict flagged by @RomneyDa. Single commit `5366ecb8` (Bartok9 authorship preserved). Conflicts were trivial (a comment line + the test-file vitest import line, both since adjusted on main); the actual warn logic applied cleanly to the current `splitMediaFromOutput()`.

**Re-verified on the rebased branch (real output):**

`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/media/parse.test.ts` → **60/60 passed**. `oxlint` on the two touched files → **0 warnings, 0 errors**.

Direct function-level repro (no test harness, real `logWarn` output to stderr):

```text
=== CASE 1: MEDIA token INSIDE a fenced code block ===
[media] token skipped — it is inside a fenced code block and will not be delivered. Remove the surrounding backticks/fence to send this as media.
mediaUrls: undefined
kept as visible text: true

=== CASE 2: MEDIA token OUTSIDE a fence (control) ===
mediaUrls: [ 'https://example.com/image.png' ]
```

Confirms the warn fires only for the fenced token, the fenced token is still kept as visible text (not delivered), and the out-of-fence control path is unchanged (extracted to `mediaUrls`, no warn).


---

## Update 2026-06-13 (2) — fixed a real `build-artifacts` regression in this PR

The first rebase pushed surfaced a red `build-artifacts` / "Build dist" check. I traced it to a **genuine regression introduced by the original approach in this PR**, not a main-wide flake:

- `src/media/parse.ts` is bundled into the **browser UI** (`ui/src/ui/chat/message-normalizer.ts` imports `splitMediaFromOutput`).
- The original patch added `import { logWarn } from "../logger.js"` directly in `parse.ts`. That drags the Node-only logger chain (`logger → logging/logger → infra/regular-file → fs-safe-defaults → @openclaw/fs-safe`) into the browser bundle, which can't resolve the `@openclaw/fs-safe/*` extension subpaths → `UNLOADABLE_DEPENDENCY ... No such file or directory`.

**Proof it was ours (local `node scripts/ui.js build`):**

| `parse.ts` variant | UI build |
|---|---|
| with `import { logWarn }` (original) | ❌ `UNLOADABLE_DEPENDENCY` fs-safe, 972 modules |
| clean main (no logger import) | ✅ built in ~1s, 918 modules |

**Fix:** keep `parse.ts` browser-pure via dependency injection. Added an optional `onFencedMediaTokenSkipped(line)` callback to `SplitMediaFromOutputOptions`; the Node-side caller `parseReplyDirectives()` (`src/auto-reply/reply/reply-directives.ts`) injects the real `logWarn`. No Node-only import in `parse.ts` anymore.

**Re-verified (`08170177b1`):**
- `node scripts/ui.js build` → ✅ built, 918 modules (matches clean main)
- `src/media/parse.test.ts` → 61/61 passing
- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-state.test.ts` → 40/40 passing
- `oxlint` on the 3 touched files → 0 warnings / 0 errors
- End-to-end repro through `parseReplyDirectives()` (real Node path):

```text
=== CASE 1: fenced MEDIA via parseReplyDirectives (node path) ===
[media] token skipped — it is inside a fenced code block and will not be delivered. Remove the surrounding backticks/fence to send this as media.
mediaUrls: undefined | kept as text: true

=== CASE 2: unfenced MEDIA (control) ===
mediaUrls: [ 'https://example.com/image.png' ]
```

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent model allowlist (with fallback to global)
target.body:
When `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, scope the visibility allowlist (used by the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` catalog view) to that agent. Otherwise fall back to `agents.defaults.models` exactly as before.

Schema:
- `agents.list[].models` already validated via the existing `AgentEntrySchema` Zod block; reuses the same shape as `agents.defaults.models` (`alias`, `params`, `agentRuntime`, `streaming`).
- gateway protocol `models.list` params: optional `agentId` (NonEmptyString).

Behavior:
- `buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` / `parseConfiguredModelVisibilityEntries` now accept `agentId`; aliases and metadata still resolve from defaults.
- gateway `server-methods/models` honors `models.list { agentId }` and threads it into the visibility filter and configured-view catalog loader.
- ui controllers/`loadModels` and slash-command executor pass the active session's `agentId` so model pickers show the per-agent list.

Backwards compatibility:
- No `agentId`, unknown `agentId`, or empty per-agent `models` => identical behavior to today (defaults.models drives the allowlist).
- `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, and provider wildcards are unchanged. `agents.list[].models` entries can also use `provider/*` wildcards.

Tests:
- `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`: 7 new cases covering per-agent allowlist, fallback to defaults when missing/unknown/empty, provider wildcards, and policy surfacing.
- `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`: end-to-end visibility scoping via the public model-selection API.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/models.test.ts`: gateway `models.list` filter with `agentId`.
- `src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts`: protocol schema accepts `agentId`.
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts`: client passes `agentId` through.
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` and `slash-command-executor.node.test.ts`: session-scoped catalog refresh forwards the agent id.

Docs:
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`: documents per-agent allowlist semantics, fallback rules, and an example config.

## Summary

- **Problem**: The visibility allowlist that powers the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view is global — driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models`. Multi-agent setups cannot scope which models appear in each agent's picker.
- **Why it matters**: With multi-agent setups (e.g. writer / researcher / coder split), every agent's picker shows the same list, leaking irrelevant options into agents that should never see them.
- **What changed**: `agents.list[<agentId>].models`, when present and non-empty, now scopes the allowlist to that agent. Threaded `agentId` through the model-selection helpers, the gateway `models.list` server method, the protocol schema, and the UI catalog loaders.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: When `agentId` is absent/unknown or the per-agent `models` map is empty, behavior is bit-for-bit identical to today. `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, provider wildcards, and any other model resolution logic remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Today the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view are driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models` (global). With this change, when `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, the picker for that agent is scoped to that list; otherwise it falls back to the global allowlist exactly as before.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw built locally from this branch (commit `5973f9d0c7`, version `2026.5.10-beta.1`) on Linux (WSL2), Node v22.22.2. Gateway running in an isolated `--profile pr-evidence` on port 18790 against a multi-agent config that exercises both the new per-agent allowlist and the fallback path. Productive install on the standard port left untouched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node openclaw.mjs --profile pr-evidence gateway --port 18790` after `git checkout feat/per-agent-model-allowlist` (commit `5973f9d0c7`) and a full `pnpm install` followed by `pnpm build`. Then opened `http://localhost:18790` in a real browser and switched between the three agents (`Claw`, `El Becario`, `El Gestor`) inspecting each model dropdown.
- **Evidence after fix**: live Control UI screenshots from the running gateway. Before / after comparison embedded inline. Before, productive install on `2026.5.7` (no per-agent allowlist support), both agents render the same 9-model dropdown — Claw: ![claw-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-becario-dropdown.png). After, built from this branch with per-agent `agents.list[].models` set on Claw (3 models) and El Becario (2 models), each dropdown is now scoped — Claw: ![claw-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **Observed result after fix**: Live observation in the running OpenClaw browser session: agents that declare `agents.list[].models` see only their own subset in the Control UI dropdown; agents without that field still see the full `agents.defaults.models` catalog. Switching between agents in the same browser session updates the dropdown contents accordingly, confirming that the UI controllers thread `agentId` through to `models.list` correctly. Concretely the Control UI rendered: `Claw` showed exactly its 3 declared models (`anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, `ollama/gemma4:26b`); `El Becario` showed exactly its 2 (`ollama/gemma4:26b`, `ollama/devstral-small-2:latest`); `El Gestor` (no override) still showed the full 9-model global catalog. Live screenshot of Claw scoped picker: ![claw-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and live screenshot of El Becario scoped picker: ![becario-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **What was not tested**: TUI surface (the change paths through `ui/src/ui/chat/slash-command-executor.ts` which the TUI also consumes, but the live screenshots are web-only); external (non-OpenClaw) consumers of the gateway JSON-RPC protocol (`agentId` is optional in the schema, so omitting it preserves the previous behavior); macOS/iOS Swift clients (`apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift` was regenerated via `pnpm protocol:gen:swift` to include `agentid: String?` in `ModelsListParams`, but a live Swift consumer test was not run).

Config used (illustrative, redacted):

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "models": {
        "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
        "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
        "ollama/qwen3-coder:30b": {},
        "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b": {},
        "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {},
        "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-haiku-4-5": {}
      }
    },
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "identity": { "name": "Claw" },
        "models": {
          "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
          "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {}
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "el-becario",
        "identity": { "name": "El Becario" },
        "models": {
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
          "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {}
        }
      },
      { "id": "el-gestor", "identity": { "name": "El Gestor" } }
    ]
  }
}
```


## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature, not a regression.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — not a regression. New behavior is covered by the unit and integration tests listed above.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config field: `agents.list[].models` (same shape as `agents.defaults.models`).
- Control UI model picker, `/model` command, and `models.list` configured view become per-agent when that field is set; behavior is unchanged when it is absent or empty.
- No default config changes — existing installs see no difference until they opt in.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
  [any agent] -> models.list (no agentId) -> defaults.models -> dropdown

After:
  [agent A with per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: A } -> list[A].models -> dropdown
  [agent B without per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: B } -> defaults.models (fallback) -> dropdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No** (the new field narrows visibility further; it never broadens access)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: Linux (WSL2, kernel 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.7 local install
- Model/provider: tested with anthropic, openai-codex, ollama, claude-cli entries
- Integration/channel (if any): webchat (Control UI)
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with `agents.defaults.models` populated and one agent overriding via `agents.list[].models`.

### Steps
1. Apply patch, build, run tests.
2. Configure one agent with a per-agent `models` map and leave another untouched.
3. Switch agents in Control UI and inspect the model dropdown contents.

### Expected
- Agent with override: dropdown lists only the agent's models.
- Agent without override: dropdown lists `agents.defaults.models`.

### Actual
- Matches expected. All targeted test suites pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new tests added; pre-existing suites unchanged)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: agent-with-override shows scoped list; agent-without-override shows defaults; unknown `agentId` falls back to defaults; empty per-agent `models` falls back to defaults; provider wildcards (`provider/*`) work in both global and per-agent maps.
- **Edge cases checked**: missing `agents.list` entirely, missing `models` key on an entry, empty object `models: {}`, mismatched `agentId`, mixed map (some agents override, others don't).
- **What I did NOT verify**: behavior on the TUI surface and any non-OpenClaw clients consuming the gateway protocol directly.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address in this PR.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No** (new field is purely additive and optional)
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A user populates `agents.list[].models` expecting it to be additive on top of defaults.
  **Mitigation**: Documented in `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` that the per-agent map *replaces* the visible set for that agent (aliases and shared metadata are still inherited from defaults).

The PR adds gateway startup trace monitor cleanup on startup failure and adjusts Google Chat and Matrix tests for stricter Response/error expectations.

PR surface: Source +5, Tests 0. Total +5 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main leaves the startup trace monitor enabled until `ready`, and the PR body supplies terminal after-fix proof for a fatal invalid-config path; the remaining patch defect is source-reproducible through recoverable measured channel startup errors.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A narrow automated repair is plausible because the blocker is confined to startup trace lifecycle cleanup and focused regression tests.

target.title: fix(gateway): disable startup trace monitor on startup failure
target.body:
## Summary
- add an explicit `startupTrace.close()` cleanup path in gateway startup tracing
- call `startupTrace.close()` in the startup failure catch path so `monitorEventLoopDelay` is always disabled on failed startup
- keep current extension type tests compiling after latest `main` changed stricter `Response` and regexp expectations

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway startup tracing with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` no longer leaves the event-loop delay monitor enabled when startup fails before readiness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of this PR branch at `e81371fe6a0bbc8a84d5c1a51a7a821611f268fa`, running Node through the repo's `tsx` loader against a real temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created a temporary invalid `openclaw.json`, then ran:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1 timeout 20s node --import tsx -e 'import("./src/gateway/server.impl.ts").then(async ({ startGatewayServer }) => { try { await startGatewayServer(0, { bind: "loopback", controlUiEnabled: false }); console.log("unexpected startup success"); process.exitCode = 2; } catch (error) { console.log("startup failed as expected"); console.log(String(error instanceof Error ? error.message : error).split("\n")[0]); } })'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
  ```text
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.file 0.5ms total=26.7ms eventLoopMax=9.9ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.hash 0.2ms total=47.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.parse 0.3ms total=49.3ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read 28.1ms total=53.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot 30.6ms total=55.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  startup failed as expected
  Invalid config at /tmp/openclaw-proof-4texI2/openclaw.json.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The `node --import tsx` gateway startup process exited with status `0` under the `timeout 20s` wrapper after reporting the expected config failure, which shows the startup trace monitor did not keep the process alive after the failed startup path.
- **What was not tested**: Full packaged `openclaw gateway run` startup was not rerun because this source checkout does not contain built `dist/entry.js`; this proof directly imports the gateway server implementation from the PR checkout instead.

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts`

target.title: feat(plugins): add Control UI entry points
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Plugins can expose gateway-authenticated HTTP routes, but there is no generic way for a plugin to register a first-class Control UI navigation entry.
- Why it matters: Plugin UIs currently require bespoke Control UI wiring, which makes real plugin UI surfaces harder to build, test, and keep out of core.
- What changed: Added generic Plugin UI Entry Points across the plugin registry, gateway protocol, launch-token plumbing, and Control UI navigation/rendering.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add the Session Search plugin or any plugin-specific UI. A separate proof plugin lives on [`Ittiz/openclaw@forge/session-search-plugin`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/tree/forge/session-search-plugin).

## Working Example Plugin

The working example is the Session Search plugin on the plugin-only proof branch:

- Branch: [`Ittiz/openclaw@forge/session-search-plugin`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/tree/forge/session-search-plugin)
- README: [`README.md`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/README.md)
- Entry point registration: [`index.ts`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/index.ts)
- Manifest: [`openclaw.plugin.json`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/openclaw.plugin.json)

That branch intentionally contains only the plugin files, not the OpenClaw source tree. It demonstrates a real app-nav Control UI plugin that searches, inspects, injects, and resumes prior sessions through the entry-point API added by this PR.
